summary.category: Current, Potential Difference, and Ohm's Law
points: Electric current (I = Q/t) is the rate of charge flow; potential difference (V = W/Q) is the work done to move unit charge between two points.
Ohm's Law states that V = IR, representing a linear relationship between voltage and current in a conductor at constant temperature.
Resistance depends on length, cross-section, and resistivity; equivalent resistance is the sum in series (Rs = R1+R2) and the reciprocal sum in parallel (1/Rp = 1/R1+1/R2).
Parallel circuits are advantageous for domestic use as they allow independent operation of gadgets and reduce over-all circuit resistance.
Joule's Law of Heating (H = I^2Rt) describes how electrical energy is dissipated as heat, utilized in devices like filaments and safety fuses.
Electric power (P = VI) is the rate of energy consumption; the commercial unit is kilowatt-hour (1 kWh = 3.6 x 10^6 J).
